xep.header:
Emoji Markup This specification leverages &xep0394; and &xep0447; to send custom emojis &LEGALNOTICE; xxxx ProtoXEP Standards Track Standards Council
XMPP Core XEP-0001 XEP-0030 XEP-0115 XEP-0231 XEP-0394
NOT_YET_ASSIGNED
markup
Snit Guckfung snit@cock.li snit@isekai.rocks
Sigrid Einarsson techmetx11@disroot.org techmetx11@disroot.org
0.0.3 2026-04-05 techmetx11
Changed the id attribute to refer to the first hash of the media element. Removed packs, and some notes referring to BoB
0.0.2 2026-04-04 techmetx11
Added emoji packs, and switched to using SFS instead of just BoB
0.0.1 2026-03-08 snit
First draft.

section1.p: Many legacy platforms allow users to create and send custom emojis in place of the ones supported by Unicode, allowing users a wider range of expression when chatting. This specification provides a way for users to send custom emojis using a custom &xep0394; element containing &xep0231; data.
Implementations which support custom emoji markup MUST advertise 'urn:xmpp:markup:emoji:0' via &xep0115; and &xep0030;.

To send a custom emoji, implementations use a custom <emoji /> element qualified with the 'urn:xmpp:markup:emoji:0' namespace inside a &xep0394; <span /> element as a child of a &xep0394; <markup /> element, with a media element (either &xep0447; <file-sharing /> or &xep0385; <media-sharing />) as a sibling of the <markup /> element. The <span /> MUST contain a 'start' and 'end' range which marks the range of text to replace with the custom emoji. If the 'start' and 'end' contain the same index, the emoji MUST be inserted at that index without replacing any text. the <emoji /> MAY contain a 'name' attribute containing the name of the emoji, but it MUST include one or more &xep0300; <hash /> elements to identify the specific emoji.
section1.example: To be, or not to be, that is the question 🤔 ENeyvkxcfv8dmL4HBrF3JU1OX1BfpNV3YbhlEb20ReU= image/png pondering 6129 64 64 ENeyvkxcfv8dmL4HBrF3JU1OX1BfpNV3YbhlEb20ReU= QdJufo3MnaEPCK/2Q8fCiX3FutiQej6uHg5HaCliheY=

example.caption: User sends multiple custom emojis in a message

section1.example: Look at this funny image I found! :laughing: I wonder what it means? :pondering: jMbLbIw1lfDhNVrE1wtB8C1R94OXKbyq/i8DUmHiHs8= a5wIaBMtKT+Rs4TSc0C4ciWb5bmA3GCBv3xIUBxpKGA= ENeyvkxcfv8dmL4HBrF3JU1OX1BfpNV3YbhlEb20ReU= QdJufo3MnaEPCK/2Q8fCiX3FutiQej6uHg5HaCliheY= image/png laughing 8391 64 64 jMbLbIw1lfDhNVrE1wtB8C1R94OXKbyq/i8DUmHiHs8= a5wIaBMtKT+Rs4TSc0C4ciWb5bmA3GCBv3xIUBxpKGA= image/png pondering 6129 64 64 ENeyvkxcfv8dmL4HBrF3JU1OX1BfpNV3YbhlEb20ReU= QdJufo3MnaEPCK/2Q8fCiX3FutiQej6uHg5HaCliheY=

section1.p: Many custom emojis can be adequately represented in terms of an existing Unicode emoji. Clients might find it useful to allow users to optionally specify a replacement emoji, which will improve the experience of users of clients which do not support custom emojis, with the name/shortcode of the emoji used only as a fallback. However, this specification makes no recommendation on the fallback text an implementation opts to use.
Implementations SHOULD expect to handle animated emojis, such as GIFs or animated PNGs. This could include either playing the animation or displaying the first frame.
Implementations might receive a file type which they do not or cannot support, in which case they MAY opt to ignore the emoji markup altogether.
Implementations MAY use sticker packs in &xep0449; for emojis too, and they MAY also scale down the emoji to the UI size if it's too large to display in-line.

Implementations SHOULD provide a proper fallback for custom emojis, as well as including the 'name' attribute. This helps both users of unsupported clients as well as users who may not be able to view the emoji properly.
Implementations MAY post-process the file for color deficiency, epilepsy, or other accessiblity reasons. This could include slowing down playback or changing the saturation.
Emojis are just media files. All the usual security risks of allowing users to send arbitrary media apply.
